seed: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

transcript.content: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.
It sounds like you’re worried that sharing would make others feel weighed down. What evidence do you have that sharing would actually burden them?

I guess I just... I don't want to add to their problems, you know? It feels like everyone has their own struggles, and I don’t want to be a reason they feel more overwhelmed. Sometimes I think if I just keep things to myself, it’ll be easier for everyone. But then, I end up feeling even more alone.

It sounds like you’re worried that opening up might add to their load.  
What evidence do you have that sharing would actually make them feel more overwhelmed?  
Could you think of a time when talking about something helped you or someone else feel less alone?

I just don’t want to be a burden. Everyone has their own issues, and I feel like I’d just add to the weight they’re carrying. It’s like, if I keep quiet, maybe it’ll be easier for them. But then I feel so isolated, like I’m just drifting away.

It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy worry that you might be adding to others’ struggles. Have you noticed moments when sharing has actually helped them or even made you feel less alone? Maybe we can explore ways to test that idea together.

Yeah, I guess there’ve been times when I did open up, and it felt sort of good, like a little relief. But then I just get this rush of anxiety, thinking I shouldn’t have said anything at all. It’s like I keep second-guessing myself, and I end up feeling guilty for even feeling that way.
It sounds like you’re worried that speaking was a mistake. What evidence do you have that what you said was wrong, and what evidence suggests it might have been okay?

I don't know... sometimes it feels like people just don't know how to respond or they change the subject. But then there are moments when they seem to care, like they really want to help. It’s confusing because I can’t really tell if I’m being dramatic or if it’s actually significant.

It sounds like you’re unsure whether your reactions are overblown or truly meaningful. What clues do you notice that point toward one view or the other? How do those clues fit with what you’ve experienced before?

Yeah, it’s tough to sort through that. Sometimes, I’ll bring something up and notice people’s faces change, like they’re uncomfortable or don’t know what to say. That makes me feel like I’m just being too much. But then I think about times when friends have shared their own struggles, and I remember feeling connected, like it was a safe space. It’s just hard to

It sounds like you’re worried you’re overstepping when you bring up things. Can you recall specific moments when people seemed uncomfortable, and also moments when they engaged or responded positively? What might those differences tell us about how you’re perceived?

Yeah, I remember a time when I talked about feeling overwhelmed at work, and one friend kind of changed the topic quickly. It made me feel like I was being... too needy or something. But then there was another time when I shared about feeling anxious, and a different friend opened up about their own struggles, and it felt really validating. It’s just so mixed, you know? Like,

It sounds like you’re noticing how the same kind of sharing can lead to very different reactions, which can feel confusing. What do you think might be going on when you feel you’re being “too needy” after opening up? This could help us explore what that belief is really saying about how you expect others to respond.

Yeah, I guess it does feel confusing. When I think I’m being too needy, it’s like this voice in my head telling me I should be strong and handle things on my own. I start feeling like maybe I’m just burdening them with my problems, like they have enough to deal with already. But then there’s this part of me that wishes I could just be real without feeling
It sounds like you’re weighing whether opening up feels like a burden. What evidence do you have that sharing your struggles would overwhelm the people you care about?

I don’t know... it’s just this feeling I get. Like, when I see my friends stressed or busy, I think they don’t have the space for my problems too. It’s almost like I can sense their energy and it makes me pull back. But maybe that’s just me overthinking? I mean, I know we all have our stuff, but it’s hard not to feel

It sounds like you’re feeling uneasy when you see your friends busy, and that makes you pull back. Have you had moments when they were open to listening, even if just briefly? That might help you see whether the space you’re worried about is really there.

Yeah, I guess there have been times when they’ve taken the time to listen, even if it's just a quick chat. It felt different, like a little spark of connection. But then I worry about whether I’m interrupting their lives. It’s like, one moment of kindness makes me hopeful, but the next moment I’m back to feeling like I shouldn’t take up any more space.

It sounds like you’re worried that your kindness might feel like an intrusion, but it could also be a moment they appreciate. When you think about a time they listened, what did they say or do that suggested they welcomed your presence? Exploring that can help you see whether the belief that you’re “taking up space” holds up.

I remember one time I was really stressed about a project, and I texted a friend just to vent. They replied pretty quickly, saying they completely understood and even shared some of their own work struggles. It felt nice, like they really cared. But then I think about how they could’ve easily just ignored my message or rushed me off. I guess I still linger on the idea that I could
